publications.editedComment: This loader measures before it paints. Four accepted effect ids map to lazily imported Components; bubbles, fizzy bubbles, and rain occupy the viewport, while butterflies may be constrained to the first element matching `surfaceSelector`. A ResizeObserver plus window, orientation, and visual-viewport listeners keep that surface rectangle current. Unknown ids render nothing. When the Animation store stops, the last effect remains mounted behind a one-second opacity fade, after which the container disappears. Cleanup disconnects the observer, clears the fade timer, and removes every listener. The dimensions are literal: either full-screen bounds or the measured target rectangle. No animation behavior is invented here—the selected child owns the moving pixels.

Four effect pixels enter through one strict map: bubble, fizzy bubbles, rain, and butterfly animation. Each child is async-loaded, but only butterflies use the measured surface layout; the other three fill the viewport. `surfaceSelector` defaults through several known dashboard/main targets, a ResizeObserver tracks the chosen element, and resize, orientation, and visual-viewport events refresh its rectangle. Missing surfaces fall back to full screen. Store deactivation starts a one-second fade instead of snapping the effect away. Timers, observer, and listeners are all dismantled on unmount. Crisp loader boundary: validate the id, place the container, mount the child, then let the child draw.

reviewer.name: The Daisy Who Saw It Start

publications.editedComment: Day one is visible here. The tester reads only the Animation store's safe effects and current active label. One button per safe effect calls `start` with that id, a test message, and no automatic duration. The random button chooses uniformly from the same available list and does nothing when the list is empty. Stop delegates directly to the store. Button styling marks the active effect while the header reports its label or None. This Component does not define, load, or render an effect; it is a small manual control surface for proving that catalog entries can start and stop through the shared store.

A merry little summoning board. `safeEffects` supplies every named button, so the tester cannot call an id outside the store's approved catalog. A direct start verifies the id still exists, then sends the store an effect id, a `${label} test` message, and `durationMs: null`. Random start picks from that exact safe list; no entries means no prank. The Stop button calls the store's stop method, while the heading reflects the active effect's label. The tester owns no animation frame, component import, or persistence. It merely rings the bell and watches which spirit the shared Animation store declares active.

The swarm remembers several ways a screen can struggle. Reduced-motion preference turns the adaptive profile off; save-data, network hints, processor count, memory, viewport area, and pixel ratio set an initial ceiling from low through ultra. Preference-store settings still control maximum count, requested fps, size, speed, and flap timing. Each particle receives random position, direction, bob, scale, fade phase, and a wing gradient assembled from Butterfly-store colors. Frame samples can lower or raise the adaptive profile after sustained bad or healthy windows, with cooldowns preventing constant changes. Resize and visibility handlers rebuild or pause the work, and unmount cancels the frame and listeners. The butterflies stay because the Component knows when fewer memories are kinder.

This crew adjusts before the weather turns mutinous. The animation derives an adaptive performance profile from reduced-motion, data-saving and network hints, CPU count, memory, render area, and pixel ratio, then caps particle count, fps, size, and speed accordingly. User preferences remain the source for the requested maximums and may select a custom profile. Butterflies are generated with randomized motion and Butterfly-store color relationships, cached as elements, and moved by one animation loop. Sampled frame health requires several bad windows before a downgrade and healthy windows before an upgrade. Visibility and resize events are handled, and every frame/listener is cleaned up. A swarm that can reef its own sails is less likely to sink the page.

Three display tiers—small, medium, and large—show five, ten, and twenty-five dollar prices, but the source explicitly says those values are not trusted for charging. Clicking a tier sends only its id to `cartStore.topup`; the server revalidates the tier and price. While the request runs, every tier button is disabled and the redirecting message appears. A failed store result exposes its message or a local fallback. On success the store redirects the browser to Stripe Checkout, so this Component performs no balance mutation or payment confirmation itself. The loading flag is released in `finally`, including unsuccessful calls. Simple chain of command: choose tier, request checkout, let the trusted server decide the numbers.

Three mana packages are charted in the interface, but their prices are display coordinates, not authority. The chosen `tier.id` sails to `cartStore.topup`, whose server route rechecks the tier and amount before returning a checkout destination. All tier buttons lock while that request is underway; the page announces the redirect and surfaces a failed result's message. A successful call leaves navigation to the store's Stripe redirect, while `finally` lowers the loading flag if the page remains. No payment data, mana credit, webhook result, or receipt is handled in this vessel. It launches checkout and reports failure—nothing more grandiose.

One control, one redirect. The button presents the Google icon and label, then `loginWithGoogle` logs the intended OAuth destination and assigns `window.location.href` to `/api/auth/google`. There is no local token exchange, popup, loading state, provider response, or user-store mutation in this Component. The server route owns the authentication flow after navigation begins. Safety remains on: this source proves only that clicking leaves the current page for the application's Google-auth endpoint. Everything beyond that boundary must be verified in the route and callback implementation, not inferred from the logo.
